channel_name: Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy
video_title: Uber CEO on AI, Autonomous Vehicles, and the Future of Transportation
gist: Dara Khosrowshahi lays out Uber's AV strategy as a supply-aggregation play: partner with (not build) 30+ AV makers, own the demand and the fleet-ops/financing/insurance ecosystem, and treat AV as 'another trillion dollar marketplace.' He shares hard unit-economics (AVs on Uber's network run 30%+ busier; >$10B FCF on >10B trips; Uber One at 50M members growing 50%; Reserve at >$5B run-rate) and frames capital allocation as organic growth and AV commitments first, buybacks second.

themes.theme: AV strategy: supply aggregation over fleet ownership

insights.claim: Uber's AV thesis is to be the demand aggregator and go-to-market layer for digital-driver makers rather than build its own driver, partnering with 30+ AV companies including Waymo, Nuro, Lucid, Nvidia, Wayve, Wabi, and Pony.ai.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Frames UBER as a picks-and-shovels demand layer that wins regardless of which AV maker leads, de-risking the AV transition for the equity.
insights.quote: we've got over 30 partnerships now with incredible uh partners like a Whimo to a neuro and lucid to an Nvidia

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi argues the AV market will mirror foundation models — no single winner, many players plus open-source/smaller models — so an aggregator that secures the most supply is structurally advantaged.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Implies AV economics won't concentrate into one vertically integrated owner, supporting the bull case for a neutral demand platform like UBER.
insights.quote: just like you're seeing in the foundation model space there isn't going to be a single winner there are going to be many players

insights.claim: Uber is building the full AV support ecosystem — depots, charging, fleet partners, financing, and autonomous insurance — and is actively collecting street data to feed partners' models so demand is instant at launch.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: These services create switching costs and capture margin beyond a pure marketplace take-rate, widening UBER's AV moat.
insights.quote: we're building the entire ecosystem so someone who builds a driver can get those cars in the road and then uh we have all the supporting infrastructure

insights.claim: The single thing Uber controls that could cause it to lose the AV opportunity is failing to secure access to AV supply — which is why it has a partnership with 'basically every AV provider' across mobility, delivery, and freight.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Identifies supply exclusivity/breadth as the key competitive battleground to monitor for UBER's AV thesis.
insights.quote: Access to supply and that's why we are you know we have a partnership with basically every AV provider whether it's in mobility or delivery or even freight

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi frames Uber–Waymo as 'coexistence' rather than pure competition, analogizing to OTAs working with hotels/airlines who also build direct channels but need incremental demand to fill capacity.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Suggests even AV makers with direct apps will keep cars on Uber's network for utilization, blunting the 'Waymo disintermediates Uber' bear case.
insights.quote: It's a coexistence that frankly I'm quite used to coming from the travel business

themes.theme: AV unit economics and cost-down trajectory

insights.claim: AVs operating on Uber's network are 30%+ busier than AVs not on the network, measured in trips and revenue per vehicle per day, materially improving ROI on expensive AV hardware.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A concrete utilization edge Uber can sell to AV fleet owners; the 30% delta is the core ROI argument for putting cars on UBER vs. running them solo.
insights.quote: AVs that are on our network are 30% or more busy than let's say one PVs who aren't using our network

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi expects AV hardware cost to fall ~30-40% per generation, citing a Lucid/Nuro midsize vehicle Uber is co-developing at roughly $60-70K, which he says is low enough to drop transportation cost for the mass market.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the AV cost curve; the $60-70K per-vehicle target is a checkpoint for whether AV ride economics can scale beyond premium urban use.
insights.quote: the lucid midsize that they are building for us and neuro together it would be 60 $70,000 car

insights.claim: Each AV vehicle drives roughly three to four times what a human driver does, so a single AV's effective throughput is substantially higher than a human-operated car.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Implies far fewer vehicles are needed to serve demand, a double-edged dynamic that could constrain manufacturing scale needed to drive unit costs down.
insights.quote: Each of these vehicles probably drives three to four times what a human does

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi invokes the original Uber experience — the market was sized to the taxi market but Uber became multiple times larger — to predict AV will similarly expand the total transportation market as cost falls and demand rises elastically.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Supports a TAM-expansion narrative for UBER under AV rather than cannibalization of existing rides.
insights.quote: When Uber was introduced early on, people were sizing the market based on the taxi market. We're multiple times bigger than the taxi market.

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi sizes the AV opportunity as 'another trillion dollar marketplace' on top of Uber's existing business.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Anchors the long-term bull TAM for UBER's AV optionality.
insights.quote: We think it's another trillion dollar marketplace.

insights.claim: Traditional OEMs are now investing in L4-ready systems because they see Level-4 autonomy as much closer than they thought; Khosrowshahi expects legacy automakers to reach it in 2-4 years.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals AV supply will broaden from startups to legacy auto over a 2-4 year window, a timeline for AV-exposed auto and supplier names.
insights.quote: I think you'll see the traditional players get there over the next, you know, 2 to four years

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi says Chinese manufacturing capability and bill-of-materials cost for AVs is 'unrivaled,' and a low-cost Foxconn-style AV contract manufacturer exists in China while the West is still working on one.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Flags a Western AV-manufacturing cost gap vs. China — a strategic and geopolitical risk/opportunity for Western AV scaling and a potential cost disadvantage.
insights.quote: The the Chinese capabilities in terms of manufacturing both in terms of quality and cost at this point is unrivaled.

insights.claim: AV production is currently in the hundreds-to-thousands of vehicles and must ramp through tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands before costs fully come down.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Sets the scale milestones to watch before AV unit economics reach mass-market thresholds.
insights.quote: right now the cars are being manufactured in the hundreds and the thousands. It'll get to the tens of thousands. It'll get to the hundreds of thousands.

insights.claim: The binding constraint on delivery drones is battery density — the battery's ability to lift itself plus payload while meeting range and recharging needs — not regulation or software.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Points investors to battery-density progress (not air-traffic rules) as the gating metric for drone-delivery economics.
insights.quote: The biggest issue is just um battery density and the ability of essentially battery to lift itself and then lift the payload

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi expects food and grocery drone delivery to hit real scale over the next two to five years, initially costing more than human delivery, eventually cutting delivery times to 10-15 minutes from 25-30.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A 2-5 year horizon and an explicit 'costs more first' caveat temper near-term drone-delivery economics for UBER and delivery peers.
insights.quote: drones for food and other grocery are going to start hitting real scale over the next two to five years

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi singles out Zipline as an interesting company and notes Joby is building passenger drones, while saying drones won't be a big part of daily life two years out but will normalize over 5-10 years.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Names specific drone players worth tracking and dampens near-term hype with a 5-10 year normalization timeline.

insights.claim: The Middle East (Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Saudi Arabia) is moving fastest on AVs with entrepreneurial regulators and no-safety-driver services live today, while New York and Boston will take longer.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Regulatory posture, not technology, dictates AV geography; Gulf and Sun Belt markets lead, mapping where AV revenue scales first.
insights.quote: Middle East is going fast... they are on it. The regulators are very entrepreneurial.

insights.claim: Uber expects to launch commercial robotaxi pilots in Europe, including London, before the end of the year, as European regulators and OEMs move to avoid being left behind.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A near-term, datable catalyst (London robotaxi pilot by year-end) for UBER's AV narrative in Europe.
insights.quote: I think we will have pilots for example in London before the end of the year

insights.claim: In Austin and Atlanta, where Uber runs two large Waymo partnerships, human drivers are making more money and driver sign-ups are increasing, suggesting AVs are adding incremental demand rather than displacing drivers early.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Early evidence against the 'AVs gut driver supply/earnings' thesis; a key data point to track as AV penetration deepens.
insights.quote: drivers in Austin and Atlanta where we have two big partnerships with Whimo, drivers on the Uber platform, they're they're making more money

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi's biggest premortem risk for the AV opportunity is public/regulatory backlash — AI and AVs are unpopular with the general public over job and cost fears — requiring Uber to move at 'the pace society is prepared for.'
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Names social/regulatory acceptance — not technology — as the top tail risk to the AV thesis for the whole industry.
insights.quote: how unpopular it is with the general public... otherwise there will be a backlash

themes.theme: AI internal adoption, cost, and the token economics squeeze

insights.claim: Uber blew through its entire annual AI budget in a single quarter, forcing it to meter headcount increases as AI-boosted engineer throughput rises.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Concrete signal that frontier-model inference costs are large and real; AI productivity is being traded against slower hiring — a margin and opex dynamic to watch.
insights.quote: We blew through our AI budget in a quarter, you know, for the whole year essentially.

insights.claim: Uber's AI playbook is to explore with expensive frontier models (OpenAI, Claude) then migrate scaled experiences to cheaper, more token-efficient or open-source models to control cost.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A reusable 'explore expensive, scale cheap' framework; bullish for cheaper/open-source inference demand once use cases productionize.
insights.quote: we're using the more expensive models to explore... once we scale some of these experiences... we'll look to bring in more efficient models that are more efficient on a token basis or open source

insights.claim: Uber engineers in India are now driving 10x the code commits they used to by using autonomous agents, with AI uptake appearing unpredictably across the org.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A tangible developer-productivity data point supporting the AI-coding thesis and the case for slower engineering headcount growth.
insights.quote: we've got devs in India who all of a sudden are driving 10x the code commits that they used to

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi pushes teams to rebuild processes from first principles with AI rather than merely optimizing 20-30% of an existing process, arguing ground-up redesign is where the real value is.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Distinguishes incremental AI gains from transformational ones — a lens for judging which companies will actually re-rate from AI.
insights.quote: to rebuild it from the ground up, that's much harder... I'm coming in and really pushing the teams to think about processes from a first principles standpoint

insights.claim: Uber's feed and search models are now ~10,000x bigger than its older models, enabling universal search across rides/eats/grocery and predicting a rider's destination ~75% of the time as a one-tap interaction.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Quantifies how larger models drive conversion/utilization; the 75% destination-prediction stat is a concrete monetization lever for UBER.
insights.quote: today we can guess when you take an Uber ride. 3/4 of the time we can guess where you're going and it's just a one tap interaction

insights.claim: Uber One has 50 million members growing 50% year-on-year and is positioned 'like Netflix' — more content for the same price (mobility discounts, surge protection, free delivery, 10% back on hotels).
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Membership scale and growth rate are a core retention/margin engine; the 50M/50% figures are key KPIs for the UBER bull case.
insights.quote: we have a Uber 1 membership program, 50 million members now, growing 50% year-on-year

insights.claim: Uber One members are unprofitable in their first year — Uber loses money on a member's early transactions — but become more profitable over years 2-4, mirroring Amazon Prime's variable-cost membership economics.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Explains why membership growth can depress near-term margins while building long-term LTV — important for reading UBER's reported profitability.
insights.quote: the first year of membership is a year where we lose money on you, but we're going to make money on you 2, three, four years from now

insights.claim: About 13% of Uber Eats bookings now come from the mobility business, giving Uber Eats a structural free-customer-acquisition advantage over pure-play delivery competitors.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the cross-platform CAC advantage that lets Uber Eats win #1 internationally with higher margins than single-product rivals like DoorDash.
insights.quote: about 13% of Eats bookings are actually coming from the mobility business

insights.claim: Uber's cross-platform flywheel is its structural edge: more services drive more interaction and retention, letting it reach #1 in Eats internationally with higher margins than single-product competitors.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: The multi-product retention loop is the durable competitive advantage underpinning UBER's margin and share gains vs. mono-line peers.
insights.quote: the platform for us is a structural advantage we have versus other model line players. We get a bunch of free customers uh from our mobility business.

insights.claim: Uber has only signed up an estimated 40-50% of the serviceable restaurants/merchants in cities where it operates, leaving substantial selection-driven growth runway in Eats.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Implies meaningful merchant-selection upside remains in core markets, a tailwind for Eats GBV growth.
insights.quote: probably signed up around 40 to 50% of the restaurants and or merchants that can be on our platform

themes.theme: Supply-first growth model and underpenetrated markets

insights.claim: Uber is fundamentally a supply company — recruiting drivers, merchants, and couriers first, after which demand follows — the inverse of the demand-first model Khosrowshahi ran at Expedia.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Reframes UBER's growth engine around supply density; investors should track supply acquisition (drivers/merchants) as the leading indicator.
insights.quote: we are a supply company... the first thing we do is actually go out and recruit drivers... and then just the demand shows up

insights.claim: A significant growth vector is expanding supply beyond top cities into sparse suburban and smaller markets — the 'next 50' and 'next 200' cities rather than the top 10.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Geographic density expansion into less-penetrated markets is a concrete, underappreciated growth lever for UBER.
insights.quote: Not the top 10 cities in a country but the next 50 cities, the next 200 cities

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi emphasizes drivers/couriers/merchants experience P95 bugs weekly (vs. monthly for consumers) because they're on the app 6-10 hours a day, so supplier-side reliability is a higher-stakes quality lever than consumer UX.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Supplier retention hinges on tail-latency reliability; an operational edge that compounds supply density and the marketplace moat.
insights.quote: A P95 bug is happening every single week for a driver who's on the app, you know, 6 hours a day

themes.theme: New verticals: hotels, travel, and on-demand-to-planned

insights.claim: Uber is entering hotels via an Expedia supply deal, passing the vast majority of the economics back to Uber One members (10% off hotels, 20% off 10,000 hotels) to deepen platform integration rather than maximize take.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Hotels are a member-retention play, not a margin grab; signals Uber will keep using new verticals to feed Uber One LTV.
insights.quote: we happened to make a deal with Expedia, my my old company, and we're essentially giving the vast majority of the economics of that deal back to Uber 1 members

insights.claim: Uber Reserve has scaled to over $5 billion run-rate from nothing 5-6 years ago by re-engineering the backend to 99%+ pre-booked reliability, proving riders will pay more for reliability and that Uber can move its brand from on-demand to planned.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A $5B+ run-rate validates the pre-commit/planned-demand expansion that underpins the travel/hotels push — a tangible monetization proof point.
insights.quote: reserve now is over $5 billion run rate. Um it didn't exist you know five six years ago

insights.claim: Uber completed 1.5 billion trips for people outside their home city last year and ~15% of all trips are to/from airports, letting it identify travelers and justify the travel/hotels expansion.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: The traveler-identification data (1.5B out-of-city trips, 15% airport mix) is the strategic rationale and asset behind UBER's move up the travel stack.
insights.quote: we completed 1 and a.5 billion trips for people outside of their home city. About 15% of our trips are to and from airports.

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi sees an idealized travel end-state where Uber auto-orchestrates the full trip (pre-booked airport rides, hotel arrival heads-up, phone-as-room-key) by reading flight/email data, turning Uber into an end-to-end in-market travel layer.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Outlines a long-term travel-superapp ambition that, if realized, materially expands UBER's wallet share per traveler.

themes.theme: Interface shift: from apps to agentic, personalized UX

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi predicts inbound user interaction will move increasingly away from apps toward unstructured, conversational AI in ~7 years, while apps persist for outbound info (maps, car tracking) better served visually.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals a UX paradigm shift toward agentic interfaces; relevant for how aggregators retain users when conversation replaces app surfaces.
insights.quote: the inbound interaction is going to be much more unstructured and AI makes it possible

insights.claim: AI lets Uber personalize its UI per user/context for the first time, breaking from the historical model of optimizing one interface to the population average.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Per-user UI personalization is a new conversion lever; a differentiator for scaled platforms with rich first-party data like UBER.
insights.quote: Our booking experience on Uber may not be optimized to you exactly, but it's optimized to overall averages... Now, we're going to personalize it.

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi reversed his prior view that marketing only acquires users and product surfaces features; storytelling-led marketing (Uber Teens, Reserve, grocery) is now essential to drive cross-service awareness and lifetime value.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Marketing-driven cross-sell awareness is a recognized growth lever for multi-product platforms — relevant to UBER's services-attach rate.
insights.quote: The goal isn't to get you coffee on that ride. The goal is to make you understand that you can get coffee on Uber

insights.claim: Uber generates over $10 billion in free cash flow but on well over 10 billion trips a year, which Khosrowshahi stresses makes it a low-margin business that must drive efficiency to lower rider prices and raise earner pay.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Frames UBER as high-FCF but thin-margin per trip; efficiency gains flow to price/earnings rather than margin, shaping how to value the cash flows.
insights.quote: We've got over um $10 billion in in free cash flow, which is great, but it's on well over 10 billion trips a year... we are not a high margin business.

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi prioritizes capital allocation as organic growth and AV commitments first, buybacks second — explicitly 'somewhere in between' Amazon (reinvest) and Apple (return cash).
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Sets expectations that UBER will fund AV/growth ahead of buybacks despite large authorizations — important for shareholders modeling capital returns.
insights.quote: I prioritize growth. I prioritize innovation over buybacks. Uh if you're building the company right, you'll do both.

insights.claim: Uber will make capital commitments to tens of thousands of AV vehicles but plans to financialize them (keep them off-balance-sheet via financing), exemplified by a newly announced ~$1 billion Santander financing line for EV and AV fleets.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: Shows Uber funding AV scale via partner financing rather than its own balance sheet — limiting capital intensity while still developing the market.
insights.quote: we just announced the deal with Santaare for example to finance both EVs and AVs

insights.claim: Uber Eats grew from under $1 billion in gross bookings when Khosrowshahi joined to over $100 billion now, evidence of the organic-compounding playbook (costs growing slower than revenue) he prioritizes.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A ~100x GBV scale-up validates the organic-investment-first strategy and the compounding thesis for the platform.
insights.quote: Uber Eats when I joined was uh doing under a billion dollars in gross bookings. That's over, you know, over 100 billion now.

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi argues AI has accelerated the rate of internal change in companies by ~5x, and that companies comfortable with constant change will adapt while rigid 'we do things a certain way' firms will struggle.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A framework for assessing which incumbents will win or lose in the AI era based on organizational adaptability, not just tech access.
insights.quote: with AI now, the rate of change inside companies... has accelerated by 5x

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi uses a 'companies are organisms that evolve by mutating' framework, actively seeking out internal 'troublemakers' and random interactions because firms with single processes and information flows die.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for organizational resilience and innovation — a lens for evaluating management quality in high-change environments.
insights.quote: every company is like an organism... organisms evolve by mutating. And companies that don't mutate... those are the companies that die.

insights.claim: Khosrowshahi's core decision-making lesson from Barry Diller is to get unfiltered 'ground truth' from source material, because edge comes from the P95/20% outliers, not the processed average that everyone reacts to identically.
insights.speaker: Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)
insights.so_what: An investing-adjacent edge framework: differentiated decisions come from unprocessed tail data, not consensus averages.
insights.quote: it's the filtering that gets the edge out of the story... It's often the edge that gives you an edge. It's not the the the average.
